items.fix: `main()` parses and dispatches; logic lives in importable functions.
items.why: Logic in `main()` is unreachable from tests and from other Python callers; the only way to use it is to shell out.
items.example:
```python
# BAD: open files, embed text, write index — all in main()
# GOOD: main() parses; calls indexer.build(path); returns int
```

items.mistake: Printing results to stderr (or messages to stdout).
items.fix: Results → stdout. Errors, progress, logs → stderr.
items.why: Mixing them breaks shell pipelines: `mycli list | grep foo` doesn't work if results go to stderr.
items.example:
```python
print(result)                  # stdout — pipeable
print('error', file=sys.stderr) # stderr — visible but doesn't pollute pipes
```

items.mistake: Returning `None` and relying on `sys.exit(0)` by accident.
items.why: An unhandled exception or implicit `None` becomes a hard-to-diagnose `1` from Python's defaults; you lose ability to signal specific failures.
items.example:
```python
if __name__ == '__main__':
    sys.exit(main())  # always returns an int
```

items.fix: Take inputs as args / `--flags` / env vars with sensible defaults.
items.why: Hard-coded paths kill portability and prevent CI / containerisation; users can't reuse the tool.

items.mistake: Dumping tracebacks to users for expected errors.
items.fix: Catch known exceptions; print a short, friendly message to stderr; return a specific exit code.
items.why: A traceback for 'file not found' is noise; users want one line that says what went wrong.
items.example:
```python
except FileNotFoundError as e:
    print(f'error: {e}', file=sys.stderr); return 1
```
